nameMeanings.name: Johnstone
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Scottish and English: variant of   Johnston  .

variants.name: Johnston
meanings: Scottish: habitational name, deriving in most cases from the place so called in Annandale, in Dumfriesshire. This is derived from the genitive case of the personal name John + + Middle English ton ‘town, village, settlement’ (Old English tūn). There are other places in Scotland so called, including the city of Perth, which used to be known as Saint John's Toun, and some of these may also be sources of the surname.

English: habitational name from Johnson Hall (Staffordshire), recorded as Johannestonc. 1233 and Joneston in 1314. The placename means ‘John's settlement’, from the genitive case of the Middle English personal name Johan, Jon (see John ) + Middle English ton ‘town, village, settlement’.

History: As far as can be ascertained, most Scottish bearers of this surname are descendants of John, probably a Norman baron from England, who held lands at Johnstone in Annandale from the Bruce family in the late 12th century. His son Gilbert was the first to take the surname Johnstone and their descendants later held the earldom of Annandale.

nameMeanings.name: John
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.

nameMeanings.name: De
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Short form of the various boys' names with the first syllable Ed-, especially Edward .

variants.name: Edward
meanings: From an Old English personal name derived from ēad ‘prosperity, riches’ + weard ‘guard’. This has been one of the most successful of all Old English names, in frequent use from before the Conquest to the present day, and even being exported into other European languages. It was the name of three Anglo-Saxon kings and has been borne by eight kings of England since the Norman Conquest. It is also the name of the youngest son of Queen Elizabeth II . The most influential early bearer was King Edward the Confessor ( ?1002–66 ; ruled 1042–66 ). In a troubled period of English history, he contrived to rule fairly and (for a time at any rate) firmly. But in the latter part of his reign he paid more attention to his religion than to his kingdom. He died childless, and his death sparked off conflicting claims to his throne, which were resolved by the victory of William the Conqueror at the Battle of Hastings. His memory was honoured by Normans and English alike, for his fairness and his piety. Edward's mother was Norman; he had spent part of his youth in Normandy; and William claimed to have been nominated by Edward as his successor. Edward was canonized in the 12th century, and came to be venerated throughout Europe as a model of a Christian king.

en.lifeSummary: When Sir John De Johnstone was born about 1330, in Johnstone, Dumfriesshire, Scotland, United Kingdom, his father, Gilbert de Johnstone, was 49 and his mother, Wife of Gilbert De Johnstone, was 40. He had at least 2 sons with Margaret DeGaarviach. He registered for military service in 1370. He died from 1382 to 1383, in Johnstone, Dumfries and Galloway, Scotland, United Kingdom, and was buried in Scotland, United Kingdom.

ko.Johnstone, Scotland: 영국 스코틀랜드 드프리스 앤드 갈로아이 존스톤
ko.lifeSummary: Sir John De Johnstone(이)가 대략 1330년 영국 스코틀랜드 드프리실르 존스톤에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Gilbert de Johnstone(은)는 49세였고, 그의 어머니 Wife of Gilbert De Johnstone(은)는 40세였다. 그는 Margaret DeGaarviach(와)과 적어도 두 명의 아들을 두었다. 그는 1370년에 군복무를 위해 등록했다. 그는 1382년 부터 1383년 까지 영국 스코틀랜드 드프리스 앤드 갈로아이 존스톤에서 사망했으며 영국 스코틀랜드에 묻혔다.
